course_description:
The course covers specialised aspects of molecular approaches to the study and understanding of immunology and infectious diseases at an advanced level presented in a modular form. Modules include: immunology, mechanisms of bacterial pathogenesis, viral infectious diseases and immunity, and parasitic diseases and immunity. Interactive learning is encouraged within each module. Literature reviews, poster and in class quiz assessment items are designed to reinforce knowledge gained in each module. There is no practical component in this course.

Immunology and infectious diseases are closely linked disciplines in which research at the molecular level is rapidly informing an understanding of pathogenic microorganisms, the immune system, and disease control. Immunology involves the study of how the body responds and defends itself from disease-causing microorganisms. This course uses important bacterial, viral, and parasitic models as examples to provide a platform for the understanding of the molecular basis of these processes and the contribution to improved disease control.

course_aims: The course aims to explore the molecular basis of microbial pathogenicity and molecular approaches to the study and understanding of immunology and infectious diseases. Modules on immunology, mechanisms of bacterial pathogenesis, viral diseases and immunity, and parasitic diseases and immunity will be used to introduce current topics and emerging advances in these fields. These concepts will be further explored and developed through targeted library assignments, quizzes and a presentation.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify key immune cells/pathways and evaluate their function in the context of health and disease.
Provide critical insight into the mechanisms and molecular basis of bacterial pathogenesis.
Understand and evaluate the interaction between selected viruses and the immune system.
Demonstrate an in-depth knowledge of selected parasitic diseases and their interaction with the immune system.
Demonstrate oral and written presentation skills based on analysis of published literature.

assessment_details.title: Module 4 Viral Diseases Oral Presentation
assessment_details.task_description: Students will submit a 5-6 min recorded presentation (students will appear in the video and use their own voice) on viral vaccine strategies. More detail on this task will be provided within the lectures and on Blackboard. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Recorded Oral presentation submitted via Blackboard
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension applications must be received no later than 24 hours after the published assessment due date and time.
Read the information contained in the following links carefully before submitting an application for extension to assessment due date.
For guidance on applying for an extension, information is available here .
For the policy relating to extensions, information is available here (Part D).
Information on medical certificates
Please note the University's requirements for medical certificates here .
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date. For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period). 10% will be deducted per day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point your submission will receive a mark of zero (0) unless an extension has been approved.
In most instances one or more hurdles will apply to your assessment item so you will need to submit it to fulfil the requirements of the course regardless of how late it is and the mark you are likely to be awarded.

assessment_details.title: Module 5 Poster
assessment_details.task_description: Students, divided into two streams, will be required to submit a poster online via Turnitin. The poster presentation will be marked individually and submitted online for assessment. The topics for the poster presentation and the task will be outlined in week 1 of this module by the lecturer. Instructions for the format of the poster presentation will be provided on the MICR7002 Blackboard site and by the lecturer. learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 3 (23 Feb - 15 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Module 1 (Immunology) Course introduction and a series of lectures covering innate/adaptive immunity.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 12 (23 Feb - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Workshops A series of contact hours will be scheduled to complete quizzes and the oral presentation task

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 5 (09 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Module 2 (Parasitic Diseases and Immunity) A series of lectures looking at parasites and their interaction with the immune system

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 6 To Week 8 (30 Mar - 26 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Module 3 (Bacterial Pathogenesis) A series of lectures covering bacterial pathogenesis.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 8 To Week 10 (20 Apr - 10 May)
learning_activities.topic: Module 4 (Viral Diseases and Immunity) A series of lectures looking at viruses and their interaction with the immune system

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 10 To Week 13 (04 May - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Module 5 - Human and Animal disease streams Either human or animal disease is chosen by students as their Module 5 stream
